desc: LA's largest grasshopper — up to 2.5 in. Gray-green, strong flier. Forms loose swarms. Desert scrub to urban gardens.

fm.Color: Gray-green; dark dorsal stripe; yellow-green when young
fm.Behavior: Strong flier; forms loose groups; can be crop pest
fm.Habitat: Desert, chaparral, gardens — any open area

hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. Preys on grasshopper. Desert (5 in).

desc: Medium brown spur-throat grasshopper. One of the most abundant and economically significant grasshoppers in California. Major crop pest of alfalfa, rangeland, and gardens. Can reach outbreak densities in dry years. Named for its agricultural devastation.

fm.Color: Brown with dark stripe; pale lateral stripe
fm.Behavior: Very common; can reach outbreak densities

hp: Grassland/rangeland herbivore. On alfalfa.

desc: Band-winged grasshopper that flashes pale yellow hindwings in flight, then vanishes on landing. Master of crypsis on bare ground.

fm.Color: Gray-brown (cryptic on bare ground); pale yellow HW visible in flight
fm.Behavior: Crepitating flight (crackly sound); flash-and-vanish display
fm.Habitat: Bare ground — trails, roadsides, rocky areas

hp: Open grassland. Display flights in courtship. On Fraxinus (ash).

desc: Band-winged grasshopper with spectacular red hindwings flashed during crackling display flights, then vanishing on landing (cryptic forewings match soil). Males produce snapping sounds in flight. Open rocky habitats and grasslands.

fm.Color: Dark brown-black; bright red hindwings visible in flight
fm.Behavior: Crepitating display flight; red wing flash; cryptic at rest

hp: Open grassland. Display flights in courtship. On Fraxinus (ash). Soil-dwelling.

desc: Large band-winged grasshopper. Black hindwings with pale border — conspicuous hovering display. Common on bare ground.

fm.Color: Brown-gray; black HW with pale yellow border (diagnostic in flight)
fm.Behavior: Males hover in display flight showing black wings; loud crepitation
fm.Habitat: Bare ground, roadsides, parking lots, dry paths

hp: Open grassland. Display flights in courtship. Preys on grasshopper. Acrididae.

desc: Large (35-45mm) yellow-green grasshopper with diagnostic black herringbone (chevron) pattern on hind femora. Major crop pest across western NA. Can reach outbreak densities. Distinguished from other Melanoplus by femoral chevrons and large size.

fm.Color: Yellow-green; black chevron marks on hind femora (diagnostic)
fm.Behavior: Strong flier; crop pest in outbreak years

hp: Grassland/rangeland herbivore. Preys on grasshopper. Acrididae.

desc: Cattail toothpick grasshopper — remarkably elongated and slender, resembling a grass blade. Green, aligned along stems for camouflage. Obligate marsh dweller in cattails and bulrushes — one of few grasshoppers tied to wetland habitat. Weak flier.

fm.Color: Green with pale lateral stripe; extremely slender
fm.Behavior: Sits vertically on grass stems; very cryptic

hp: Predator of grasshoppers — important rangeland biocontrol. Preys on grasshopper. Marsh/wetland.

desc: Creosote grasshopper — small silvery grasshopper found exclusively on creosote bush (Larrea tridentata). Cryptic coloration precisely matches host plant. An extreme host specialist — feeds, mates, and lives entirely on creosote. Desert habitats only.

fm.Behavior: Sits motionless on creosote; very cryptic; specialist
fm.Habitat: Desert creosote scrub — obligate on Larrea

hp: Grass; creosote. Desert habitat. Associated with Creosote Bush. On Larrea (creosote).

desc: Forked-tail katydid — bright green bush katydid common in gardens and brushy areas. Named for the forked cerci (tail) of males. Nocturnal stridulation — high-pitched buzzy song. On herbaceous plants and low shrubs. Females with sword-shaped ovipositor.

fm.Sound: Males sing at night — rapid series of lisps
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal singer; feeds on broadleaf foliage

hp: Acoustic signaler — song/stridulation important in mate location. Prey for bats and birds. In gardens and urban areas.

desc: Large, green, broad-winged katydid. Looks like a leaf. Males tick from treetops at night. Eggs laid in rows on twigs.

fm.Sound: Males produce sharp ticks/clicks at night
fm.Behavior: Arboreal; nocturnal; excellent leaf mimic

hp: Herbivore on tree and shrub foliage — oaks, citrus, garden plants. Nocturnal leaf-mimic. Prey for bats.

desc: Large green katydid with broad, angular wings closely resembling a leaf. Nocturnal singer in tree canopy. Flattened eggs laid in overlapping rows on twigs. Distinguished from California Katydid by more angular wing shape. Common in urban trees.

fm.Behavior: Arboreal; nocturnal; superb leaf mimic

hp: Acoustic signaler — song/stridulation important in mate location. Prey for bats and birds. Urban habitat.

desc: Pale green, delicate cricket whose males produce a pure-toned trill that's the quintessential summer night sound. Temperature-dependent chirp rate.

fm.Color: Pale green; translucent wings; slender; delicate
fm.Sound: Pure musical trill; chirp rate = temperature (Dolbear's Law)
fm.Behavior: Males trill from leaf edges; chew feeding holes to amplify sound
fm.Habitat: Gardens, orchards, hedges — on broadleaf foliage

hp: Omnivore. Song important in acoustic ecology. Prey for many vertebrates. Preys on cricket. Family Gryllidae.

desc: The classic chirping cricket of warm evenings. Introduced from Europe. Males produce familiar rhythmic chirp by rubbing forewings. Now commercially raised worldwide as food for reptile pets and as human protein source. Found in/around buildings.

fm.Sound: Familiar chirping — males rub wings together
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; attracted to warmth and lights; enters buildings

hp: Found in and around buildings and structures. Preys on cricket.

desc: Bizarre burrowing cricket with mole-like forelegs. Burrows through soil, damaging turf. Rarely seen above ground.

fm.Color: Brown; velvety; enlarged front legs for digging
fm.Sound: Males produce churring song from burrow entrance at night
fm.Behavior: Subterranean; mole-like forelegs dig through soil; flies to lights

hp: Omnivore. Song important in acoustic ecology. Prey for many vertebrates. Preys on cricket. Soil-dwelling.

desc: Introduced from India. Parthenogenetic — all females, no males needed for reproduction. Bright green stick insect up to 4 inches. On ivy and ornamental plants in LA gardens. Popular lab animal and classroom pet. Nocturnal herbivore.

fm.Behavior: Parthenogenetic (no males); slow-moving; excellent crypsis; drops to ground when disturbed
fm.Habitat: Urban gardens — ivy, privet, roses, ornamentals

hp: Stick insect — cryptic herbivore on shrub foliage. Twig mimic. Prey for birds.

desc: Western stick insect — native stick insect of chaparral and coastal sage scrub. Brown, shorter antennae than introduced phasmids. Feeds on chamise and other native shrubs. Cryptic — looks exactly like a dead twig. Distinguished from introduced Carausius by brown color and habitat.

fm.Color: Brown to gray-brown; twig-like; short antennae
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; hangs motionless on chaparral branches by day

hp: Coastal sage scrub and chaparral. Associated with Black Sage. In chaparral habitat. Cryptically colored.

desc: California mantis — LA's native praying mantis. Green or brown morphs. Smaller and more delicate than introduced Chinese or European mantids. Males fly to lights at night. Ambush predator on flowers and foliage. Declining — outcompeted by larger introduced species.

fm.Color: Green or brown (dimorphic); wings shorter than abdomen in ♀
fm.Behavior: Ambush predator; males fly to lights; cannibalistic mating
fm.Diet: Predator — any insect it can catch

hp: LA's native mantis — declining due to competitive displacement by introduced Tenodera sinensis (Hurd 1999). Green or brown morphs.

desc: Introduced. The classic 'praying mantis.' Larger than native species. Green with black spot inside forelegs (diagnostic).

fm.Color: Green (rarely brown); black eyespot on inner foreleg (diagnostic)
fm.Behavior: Ambush predator; sold commercially for biocontrol
fm.Season: May–Nov; egg cases on stems overwinter

hp: Ambush predator of flying insects. Beneficial garden predator.

desc: Introduced from Asia, now LA's largest mantis at up to 4 inches. Tan-green with distinctive green lateral forewing stripe. Voracious generalist predator — occasionally takes hummingbirds and small lizards. Sold as 'beneficial insect' but may harm native insects.

fm.Color: Tan to green; green lateral wing stripe
fm.Behavior: Ambush predator; catches hummingbirds at feeders occasionally
fm.Diet: Predator — large insects, occasionally small vertebrates
fm.Season: May–Nov; large egg cases on branches

hp: Generalist predator of insects and other arthropods. Visited by/prey for hummingbirds.

desc: Bordered mantis — desert mantis similar to S. californica but typically in drier, hotter habitats at desert edge. Green or brown. Males with longer wings than females. Ambush predator. Distinguished from California Mantis by habitat and subtle structural differences.

fm.Color: Green or brown; often more brownish than S. californica
fm.Behavior: Ambush predator; desert-adapted

hp: Praying mantis — ambush predator of flying insects. Beneficial garden predator. Desert habitat.

desc: LA's smallest and most easily overlooked mantis at barely 25mm. Gray-brown, ground-dwelling, found walking on soil and rocks rather than on vegetation. Cryptic against sand and gravel. Native. Desert and open scrub habitats.

fm.Behavior: Ground-dwelling; runs rather than flies; easily missed due to size
fm.Habitat: Rocky ground, sandy soil, desert scrub

hp: Praying mantis — ambush predator of flying insects. Beneficial garden predator. Desert habitat.

desc: Small, bright green katydid introduced from southern Europe. Extremely common in urban LA gardens. Nocturnal singer with soft, brief lisp. On roses, citrus, and ornamental plants. Distinguished from native Scudderia by smaller size.

fm.Behavior: Common garden katydid; nocturnal singer

hp: Mediterranean katydid — nocturnal herbivore on citrus, Rosa, and garden ornamentals. Males sing soft lisp at night.

desc: Major crop pest across western NA. Can form loose swarms during outbreak years, traveling considerable distances. Brown with diagnostic dark hind femoral bands. On grasses and crops. One of the most studied and economically significant NA grasshoppers.

fm.Description: Major crop pest across western NA

hp: Grassland/rangeland herbivore. On Poaceae (grasses).

desc: Large green grasshopper reaching 2.5 inches. Strong flier capable of sustained flight. Related to the desert locust (S. gregaria) but does not swarm. Broad diet of grasses and forbs. Distinguished from S. nitens by green (not brown) base color and heavier build. Valley and foothill grasslands.
hp: Open grassland. Display flights in courtship. Associated with Grasses.

desc: Shoshone grasshopper — large bright green grasshopper of desert margins. Strong sustained flier capable of crossing open desert. On creosote bush and mesquite. Named for the Shoshone people. Distinguished from S. obscura by habitat (desert vs. grassland).

fm.Habitat: Large bright green grasshopper of desert margins

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Associated with Creosote Bush. Desert habitat. Desert specialist.

desc: Spectacular black, red, yellow, and blue grasshopper. Flightless — relies on warning coloration. Desert margins.
hp: Open grassland. Display flights in courtship. Preys on grasshopper. Desert specialist.

desc: Common garden earwig with rear pincers. Nocturnal omnivore. Female guards eggs — rare maternal care in insects.

fm.Behavior: Nocturnal omnivore; ♀ guards eggs

hp: Predator of earwigs and soil-dwelling insects. Preys on earwig.

desc: Brown grasshopper of grasslands and dry agriculture. Can build dense populations in drought years. An enigmatic species (hence the name) — taxonomy debated. Important rangeland pest in California's Central and coastal valleys.

fm.Description: Brown grasshopper of grasslands and agriculture

hp: Grassland/rangeland herbivore. Preys on grasshopper. Coastal habitat.

desc: Pale green tree cricket whose chirp rate reliably correlates with temperature (Dolbear's Law: count chirps in 13 seconds, add 40 = °F). The 'thermometer cricket.' Nocturnal singer in trees and shrubs. Omnivorous — eats aphids and plant material.
hp: Snowy tree cricket — chirp rate predicts temperature (Dolbear's Law). Also preys on aphids on tree foliage. Nocturnal acoustic signaler.

desc: Large band-winged grasshopper with three bold dark forewing crossbands. Displays bright yellow hindwings in crackling flight. Open desert and grassland. One of the most striking band-winged grasshoppers in LA County.

fm.Description: Large band-winged grasshopper with three dark forewing bands

hp: Open grassland. Display flights in courtship. Preys on grasshopper. Three-banded.

desc: Coral-winged grasshopper — gray-brown grasshopper with diagnostic bright coral-red hind tibiae visible when jumping. Among the earliest spring grasshoppers — adults active in February-March. Open grassland and desert scrub. Distinctive red legs unmistakable.

fm.Description: Gray-brown grasshopper with bright coral-red hind tibiae

hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. Preys on grasshopper. Desert specialist.

desc: California angle-wing katydid — bright green leaf-mimic katydid. Broad wings almost perfectly replicate a leaf including veins. Nocturnal stridulation — loud rhythmic ticking from oaks and garden trees. Common throughout LA's urban tree canopy. Eggs laid on twigs.
hp: On coast live oak (quercus agrifolia). On Quercus (oak). In gardens and urban areas.

desc: Large, bald-headed, amber-brown cricket to 50mm. Nocturnal burrower in soil. Powerful mandibles can deliver a memorable bite. Called 'niña de la tierra' (child of the earth) in Spanish. Not actually from Jerusalem — name origin obscure. Drumming courtship.

fm.Behavior: Powerful jaws; drumming courtship

hp: Predator of crickets and related Orthoptera. Preys on cricket. Soil-dwelling.

desc: Carinate shieldback — robust, flightless katydid of chaparral. Males stridulate at night with short raspy calls. Omnivorous — eats plant material and other insects. Dark brown, heavy-bodied. Distinguished by prominent pronotal ridge (carina). Local to California.
hp: Acoustic signaler — song/stridulation important in mate location. Prey for bats and birds. In chaparral habitat.

desc: Silent field cricket — black cricket with familiar chirping song. Temperature affects chirp rate. Important food source for many predators — birds, lizards, spiders. Also commercially raised as pet food. Omnivorous. Males sing from burrow entrances to attract females.
hp: Predator of crickets and related Orthoptera. Prey for lizards.

desc: Small wingless stick insect on Ceanothus. Classic subject of speciation research. Some populations parthenogenetic.
hp: On ceanothus (california lilac) — native shrub specialist. Timematidae.

desc: California camel cricket — humpbacked, wingless cricket of dark spaces — caves, garages, basements, under rocks. Extremely long antennae (2x body length). Nocturnal scavenger on organic matter. Powerful jumping legs. Harmless despite alarming appearance when found indoors.

fm.Behavior: Wingless; very long antennae; jumps when disturbed

hp: Indoor trailing ant — nuisance pest. Preys on cricket.

desc: Small brown cockroach increasingly replacing the oriental cockroach across SoCal. Males can fly; females have reduced wings. Prefers outdoor habitats — leaf litter, mulch, irrigated areas. Originally from Central Asia. Less of an indoor pest than German cockroach.

fm.Behavior: Replacing oriental cockroach in SoCal

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. In leaf litter.

desc: Small tan cockroach (12-15mm), the world's most successful indoor pest insect. Rapid reproduction (30-40 eggs per ootheca, 4-6 oothecae per female). Exclusively indoor — cannot survive outdoors in most climates. Highly resistant to many insecticides.

fm.Color: Tan with two dark pronotal stripes
fm.Behavior: Rapid reproduction; nocturnal

hp: Most successful indoor pest insect globally (Schal & Hamilton 1990). Rapid reproduction. Insecticide resistance evolution documented.

desc: Large reddish-brown cockroach to 40mm. Despite name, of African origin. Called 'waterbug' in SoCal. Prefers warm moist environments — sewers, basements, restaurants. Can fly short distances in warm weather. Fastest running insect relative to size.
hp: Ecological role from description context. Preys on cockroach.

desc: Silvery wingless fish-shaped insect in bathrooms and closets. Ancient lineage — predates dinosaurs by 100 million years. Eats paper, glue, starch.

fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; very fast; eats paper and starch

hp: Orthopteran — herbivore or omnivore. Acoustic ecology and vertebrate prey base.

desc: Similar to silverfish but mottled gray-brown and heat-loving. Found near water heaters, ovens, furnaces. Prefers >90°F.

fm.Habitat: Near heat sources — water heaters, ovens
fm.Behavior: Heat-seeking; prefers >90°F

hp: Indoor detritivore — eats starch, paper, textiles, dried food. Near heat sources. Not on plants.

desc: Large shiny dark cockroach called 'waterbug.' Prefers cool damp areas. Being displaced by Turkestan cockroach in SoCal.

fm.Behavior: Slow runner; strong musty smell; declining

hp: Omnivorous scavenger. Detritivore in natural and urban habitats.

desc: Distinctive insect with elongated 'neck' raised cobra-like. A living fossil — unchanged since the Jurassic. Predatory. California endemic genus.

fm.Color: Dark brown-black; elongated prothorax; clear wings
fm.Habitat: Oak woodland — on bark of live oaks
fm.Behavior: Raises front body like a snake; predatory

hp: Generalist predator of insects and other arthropods. Predatory. California.

desc: The most commonly encountered mantis in LA gardens. Smaller than Chinese Mantis. Distinctive purple-blue eyespot on inner hindwing. Introduced from Mediterranean.

fm.Color: Green or brown; purple-blue eyespot on inner HW
fm.Habitat: Gardens, shrubs — the common LA garden mantis

hp: Praying mantis — ambush predator of flying insects. Beneficial garden predator. Garden associate.

desc: Dark wingless earwig with banded yellow-brown legs. Tropical origin. Common in gardens, greenhouses, under pots. More heat-tolerant than European Earwig.

fm.Season: Year-round; more tropical than European Earwig

hp: Indoor trailing ant — nuisance pest. Preys on earwig.

desc: Humpbacked wingless cricket in garages, basements, and crawl spaces. Extremely long antennae. Introduced from Asia. Cannot chirp. Jumps erratically when disturbed.

fm.Size: ½–¾ in body; antennae much longer
fm.Habitat: Garages, basements, crawl spaces — dark moist areas
fm.Behavior: Cannot chirp; jumps erratically when startled

hp: Omnivore. Song important in acoustic ecology. Prey for many vertebrates. Preys on cricket. Introduced species.

desc: Very large bright green katydid. Loud buzzing song audible over 200m. Introduced from Europe. Males sing from treetops. Can bite but not venomous.

fm.Habitat: Trees, tall vegetation — sings from canopy
fm.Song: Very loud continuous buzz; audible at distance

hp: Herbivore and predator on vegetation — eats leaves and insects. Loud song audible 200m.

desc: Tiny ground-dwelling grasshopper with pronotum extending past wing tips. Found on bare moist soil. Semi-aquatic — can swim. Often overlooked due to small size.

fm.Habitat: Moist bare soil, pond edges — semi-aquatic
fm.Behavior: Can swim; pronotum extends beyond wings

hp: Freshwater aquatic habitat — streams and ponds. Preys on grasshopper.

desc: Tiny elongated insect that spins silk from enlarged front tarsi — unique among insects. Lives in silk galleries on tree bark and walls. Colonial. Often overlooked.

fm.Color: Dark brown; soft-bodied; enlarged front feet
fm.Habitat: Tree bark, walls — colonial silk galleries
fm.Behavior: Spins silk from front feet (unique); lives in galleries

hp: Orthopteran — herbivore or omnivore. Acoustic ecology and vertebrate prey base.

desc: Spectacular large grasshopper — black with yellow stripes, orange-red wings. Desert species. Toxic — aposematic coloring warns predators.

fm.Warning: Toxic — produces foul secretion from thorax

hp: Predatory. Desert habitat. Warning-colored (aposematic).

desc: Tiny cricket-like insect with huge hind legs adapted for jumping on sand. Desert specialist. Nocturnal. Rarely seen.
hp: Cricket — omnivore. Song important for mate attraction. Prey for many vertebrates.

desc: Small indoor cockroach preferring dry warm locations (unlike German cockroach). Brown with two light bands across wings. Found in bedrooms, living rooms.

fm.Color: Light brown; two pale transverse bands

hp: Brown-banded cockroach — indoor pest in warm, dry areas of buildings. Prefers higher locations than German cockroach. Scavenger on starchy foods.

desc: Medium grasshopper with transparent hindwings. Pest of rangeland during outbreaks. Gregarious — can form dense swarms.
hp: Grassland/rangeland herbivore. Preys on grasshopper. Acrididae.

desc: Increasingly replacing Acheta domesticus in pet trade and escaped populations. Smaller, tan with dark crossbands. Chirps at night. Originally from SW Asia.
hp: Builds horizontal sheet/dome web. In buildings.

desc: Native western earwig — unlike the introduced European earwig, this species has toothed forceps (hence 'dentata'). Found under bark and rocks in foothill habitats.
hp: On tree bark. Part of bark-dwelling community. Preys on earwig.

desc: Invasive South African mantis established in SoCal since ~2014. Smaller and greener than native mantis species. Displacing native Stagmomantis. Females cannibalistic during mating.

fm.Color: Bright green; smaller than native mantises

hp: Ambush predator of flying insects. Beneficial garden predator.

desc: Common along chaparral and sage scrub trails. Gray-brown with distinctive hump-backed profile. Band-winged — pale blue hindwings flash in flight.

fm.Color: Gray-brown; humped profile; pale blue HW in flight

hp: Grass; sage. Chaparral. Associated with Black Sage. Associated with Ash.

desc: Bright green leaf-mimic. Common in coastal sage scrub and gardens. Sings a rapid series of soft ticks at night. Broad flat wings perfectly mimic leaves.

fm.Color: Bright green; broad flat leaf-shaped wings

hp: In coastal sage scrub habitat. Associated with Black Sage. Leaf mimic.

desc: Endemic to California chaparral. Flattened body with brown coloring matches dead leaves. Unusual wing-clapping courtship display.
hp: Native chaparral scrub habitat. In chaparral habitat. California.

desc: Small brown cricket of chaparral and sage scrub understory. Nocturnal. High-pitched continuous trill at night. Common but rarely seen.
hp: Coastal sage scrub and chaparral. Associated with Black Sage. Preys on cricket.

desc: Large bald-headed insect with mahogany-colored body. Nocturnal burrower. Powerful jaws. Drumming communication through soil vibrations.

fm.Behavior: Drums abdomen on soil for communication

hp: Predator of crickets and related Orthoptera. Preys on cricket. Soil-dwelling.

desc: Medium spur-throated grasshopper of mountain chaparral and scrub. Brownish with dark femur bars. Common in San Gabriel Mountains.
hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. Preys on grasshopper. In chaparral habitat.

desc: Band-winged grasshopper of rocky desert washes. Cryptic on pale substrate. Blue-tinted hindwings flash during flight then vanish on landing.

fm.Color: Pale gray-tan; blue-tinted HW in flight

hp: Open grassland. Display flights in courtship. Associated with Ash.

desc: Wingless. Large and robust. Nocturnal — found crossing trails at night. Omnivorous. Can deliver painful defensive bite.
hp: Acoustic signaler — song/stridulation important in mate location. Prey for bats and birds. Nocturnal.

desc: Texan spot-winged grasshopper — small grasshopper of dry grassland and scrub. Distinctive dark spot on hindwing. Crepitating (crackling) flight display.
hp: Open grassland. Display flights in courtship. Preys on grasshopper. Family Acrididae. Grassland habitat.

desc: Ashy spur-throat grasshopper — gray-brown grasshopper of chaparral and sage scrub. Common on trails. Part of the very species-rich Melanoplus genus.
hp: Grass; sage. Chaparral. Associated with Black Sage and Arizona Ash.

desc: Pacific green grasshopper — bright green grasshopper of moist grassland and riparian areas. One of the few all-green grasshoppers in LA County. Common in irrigated lawns.
hp: Predator of grasshoppers — important rangeland biocontrol. Preys on grasshopper. Riparian habitat.

desc: Dragon lubber grasshopper — large chaparral grasshopper with bizarre elongated head and spiny pronotum. Wingless. California endemic. Looks prehistoric.
hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. Preys on grasshopper. In chaparral habitat.

desc: Wheeler's mantis — native praying mantis of desert scrub. Smaller and more slender than Chinese mantis. Tan-brown coloring. Declining in areas colonized by non-native mantises.
hp: Desert and desert-edge habitats. Desert specialist. California.

desc: Intermediate-winged grasshopper — large band-winged grasshopper of desert and foothill grassland. Bright yellow hindwings with dark band. Crackling flight display.

fm.Color: Brown; yellow HW with dark band in flight

hp: Open grassland. Display flights in courtship. Preys on grasshopper. Saussure's.

desc: Ramona band-winged grasshopper — medium grasshopper of open foothill habitat. Red or orange hindwings flash during flight display, then vanish on landing.

fm.Color: Brown mottled; red/orange HW in flight

hp: Open grassland. Display flights in courtship. On Fraxinus (ash).

desc: California shield-backed katydid — dark wingless katydid of coastal sage scrub. Nocturnal. Omnivorous scavenger. Can deliver painful bite. California native.
hp: In coastal sage scrub habitat. Associated with Black Sage. Nocturnal. Scavenger.

desc: Chaparral shield-backed katydid — large wingless katydid found under rocks and bark in chaparral. Nocturnal predator/scavenger. California endemic.
hp: Cave/crevice specialist — adapted to dark, stable microhabitats.

desc: Small pale grasshopper of desert washes. Excellent camouflage on sandy substrate. Band-winged — pale hindwings visible in flight.
hp: Open grassland. Display flights in courtship. Associated with Ash.

desc: Shore band-winged grasshopper — coastal grasshopper with pale blue hindwings. Found on sandy beaches and dunes. Excellent camouflage on sand.

fm.Color: Sand-colored; pale blue HW in flight

hp: Open ground — crackling display flights. Preys on grasshopper. Beach habitat.

desc: Large dark wingless earwig of coastal and riparian habitats. Under driftwood and beach debris. Powerful pincers. One of LA County's largest earwigs. Predatory.
hp: Predatory — hunts arthropod prey. Coastal habitat. Preys on earwig.

desc: Medium grasshopper of desert scrub with distinctive white stripe. Band-winged — yellow hindwings visible in flight.

fm.Color: Brown with white lateral stripe; yellow HW

hp: Open grassland. Display flights in courtship. Preys on grasshopper. White-lined.

desc: California grasshopper — medium band-winged grasshopper of open rocky habitats. Crepitating flight display. Native California species.

fm.Color: Gray-brown mottled; pale HW in flight

hp: Open grassland. Display flights in courtship. Preys on grasshopper. Family Acrididae.

desc: Very large earwig found on sandy riverbanks and beaches. Up to 1 inch including forceps. Predatory. One of the largest earwigs in the world.
hp: Predator of earwigs and soil-dwelling insects. Preys on earwig. Beach habitat.

desc: Medium brown grasshopper of sagebrush scrub. Compressed hind tibiae (complanatipes). Common in Great Basin-adjacent habitats.
hp: Grass; sage. Associated with California Sagebrush.

desc: Small grasshopper with narrow wings. Short-winged — poor flier. Grassland and open scrub habitats.
hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. Preys on grasshopper. Grassland habitat.

desc: Bright green grasshopper specializing on snakeweed (Gutierrezia) and related composites. Sequesters plant chemicals for defense.
hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. Associated with Composites.

desc: Green grasshopper of coastal sage scrub. On sagebrush and goldenbush. Part of the species-rich California Melanoplus-group.

fm.Color: Green; on coastal sage scrub plants

hp: Predator of grasshoppers — important rangeland biocontrol. Associated with Sagebrush.

desc: Band-winged grasshopper of rocky open habitats. Blue-tinted hindwings visible in flight. Restricted to coastal mountains.
hp: Open grassland. Display flights in courtship. Preys on grasshopper. Coastal habitat.

desc: Extraordinarily elongated katydid with thread-thin legs and body. Among the most slender insects. Nocturnal in chaparral shrubs. California endemic.

fm.Note: California endemic — one of thinnest insects

hp: Chaparral habitat. Preys on insects.

desc: Delicate pale green tree cricket. Males produce melodious trilling song from shrubs and trees. Count chirps to estimate temperature.

fm.Song: Melodious trilling; chirp rate tracks temperature

hp: Omnivore. Song important in acoustic ecology. Prey for many vertebrates. Preys on cricket. Family Gryllidae.

desc: Tiny ground-dwelling mantis only 1 inch long. Brown, wingless. Ambushes prey from ground level in sage scrub and grassland. Easily overlooked.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Associated with Black Sage.

desc: Brown wingless cricket of chaparral. Found under rocks. Nocturnal. Part of the diverse camel cricket fauna of California.
hp: Predator of crickets and related Orthoptera. Preys on cricket. In chaparral habitat.

desc: Large band-winged grasshopper with pink to rose hindwings. Crepitates in flight over open rocky ground. Desert and scrub.
hp: Painted-wing grasshopper — band-winged display flight with colored hindwings. Desert and grassland edges. Herbivore on grasses and forbs.

desc: Band-winged grasshopper with bluish hindwings visible in crepitating flight. Rocky desert and scrub habitats. Flies with crackling sound.
hp: Open grassland. Display flights in courtship. Preys on grasshopper. Caerulean-winged.

desc: Medium grasshopper of coastal sage scrub. Dark hind femora. On native shrubs. Part of the diverse California grasshopper fauna.
hp: Grass; sage. Associated with Black Sage.

desc: Small pale tree cricket similar to snowy tree cricket. Males trill from shrubs at night. Named for entomologist C.V. Riley.
hp: Omnivore. Song important in acoustic ecology. Prey for many vertebrates. Preys on cricket. Family Gryllidae. Riley's.

desc: Small slender grasshopper of grassy slopes. Stridulates by rubbing hind femur against wings. Foothill and mountain meadows.
hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. Preys on grasshopper. Acrididae.

desc: Band-winged grasshopper with distinctive frontal sulcus (groove). Desert and rocky habitats. Pale hindwings visible in crepitating flight.

fm.Color: Tan-gray; groove on face; pale HW in flight

hp: Open grassland. Display flights in courtship. Preys on grasshopper. Groove-headed.

desc: Tiny wingless cricket living inside ant nests. Myrmecophile — tolerated by ants. Feeds on food shared between ants. Remarkable symbiosis.

fm.Behavior: Lives inside ant colonies; fed by ants

hp: Omnivore. Song important in acoustic ecology. Prey for many vertebrates. Preys on cricket. Ant associate.

desc: Very small ground-dwelling grasshopper (tetrigid). Under 1 cm. On bare ground near water. Among the smallest grasshoppers.

fm.Color: Brown; very small; on bare ground

hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. Preys on grasshopper. Tettigoniidae.

desc: Small band-winged grasshopper of desert pavement. Excellent camouflage on rocky ground. Named for Swiss naturalist Saussure.
hp: Open ground — crackling display flights. Preys on grasshopper.

desc: Large wingless katydid with chestnut-brown coloring. Nocturnal in chaparral. Shield-shaped pronotum. Can bite. Omnivorous scavenger.
hp: Cave/crevice specialist — adapted to dark, stable microhabitats. Nocturnal. Scavenger.

desc: Stocky short-winged grasshopper of chaparral. Flightless. Found on ground among shrubs. California native.
hp: Open grassland. Display flights in courtship. Preys on grasshopper. In chaparral habitat.

desc: Small wingless katydid of chaparral. Nocturnal. Named for entomologist E.D. Ball. Part of the California endemic shieldback fauna.

fm.Color: Brown; wingless; small for a shieldback

hp: Katydid — nocturnal foliage feeder. Song important in acoustic community. Prey for bats. Chaparral. Ball's.

desc: Very large grasshopper (3+ inches). Striped compound eyes distinctive. Introduced from Mediterranean. Strong flier. Can damage garden plants.
hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. Preys on grasshopper. In gardens and urban areas.

desc: Medium grasshopper with pale facial bristles. Foothill and mountain habitats. On native bunchgrasses.
hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. On Poaceae (grasses). Acrididae.

desc: Small tree cricket with four dark marks on antennae bases. Males trill from vegetation at night. Ground-nesting.
hp: Omnivore. Song important in acoustic ecology. Prey for many vertebrates. Preys on cricket. Gryllidae.

desc: Small grasshopper of arid scrub. Reduced wings. Ground-dwelling among rocks and desert pavement.
hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. Preys on grasshopper. Desert slender-winged.

desc: Common brown field cricket. Males chirp from burrows at night. Variable coloring — brown to black. Important food for many predators.

fm.Song: Chirps from ground burrows at night

hp: Predator of crickets and related Orthoptera. Preys on cricket. Predatory.

desc: Large dark wingless katydid of chaparral. Nocturnal. Omnivorous. Named for entomologist Morse. Can bite if handled.
hp: Katydid — nocturnal foliage feeder. Song important in acoustic community. Prey for bats. Chaparral. Morse's.

desc: Medium band-winged grasshopper of rocky mountain habitats. Crepitates loudly in flight. Well-camouflaged on volcanic rock.
hp: Open grassland. Display flights in courtship. Preys on grasshopper. Acrididae.

desc: Desert clicker — small grasshopper that produces distinctive clicking sounds while perched on creosote bush. Host-specific to creosote. Camouflaged on stems.
hp: Desert habitat. Cryptically camouflaged.

desc: Large katydid with bluntly rounded head cone. Nocturnal singer in tall grass and weeds. Loud buzzing song. Green, leaf-shaped.
hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. Nocturnal.

desc: Western camel cricket — large humpbacked wingless cricket. Nocturnal. In caves, basements, under rocks. Very long antennae. Scavenger.

fm.Color: Brown; humpbacked; wingless; very long antennae

hp: Predator of crickets and related Orthoptera. Preys on cricket. Scavenger.

desc: Large green katydid with long wings. Nocturnal singer in chaparral shrubs. Leaf-mimicking body shape. Named for entomologist Lawrence Bruner.
hp: Acoustic signaler — song/stridulation important in mate location. Prey for bats and birds. Leaf mimic.

desc: Large wingless katydid of chaparral. Shield-shaped pronotum. Nocturnal omnivore. Can bite.
hp: Katydid — nocturnal foliage feeder. Song important in acoustic community. Prey for bats. In chaparral habitat.

desc: Wingless katydid endemic to Santa Monica Mountains. Nocturnal. Conservation concern — restricted range.
hp: Conservation concern — declining habitat or population. See CONSERVATION status. Nocturnal.

desc: Timberlake's grasshopper — large desert grasshopper of the Mojave margin. On creosote bush and desert scrub. Named for early entomologist P.H. Timberlake of UC Riverside.
hp: Grass; creosote. Desert habitat. Associated with Creosote Bush

desc: Large green grasshopper with dorsal pale line. Strong flier. Related to plague locusts but does not form damaging swarms in NA.
hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. Preys on grasshopper. Acrididae.

desc: Medium grasshopper with distinctively slanted face and dorsal stripes. On bunchgrasses in dry habitats. Well-camouflaged.
hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. On Poaceae (grasses). Acrididae.

desc: Very small tetrigid on bare moist ground. Among the smallest grasshoppers. Feed on algae and mosses rather than higher plants.
hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. Preys on grasshopper. Acrididae.

desc: Small colorful grasshopper with distinctive red lines on body. On desert scrub vegetation. One of the more strikingly marked grasshoppers.
hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. Preys on grasshopper. Red-lined.

desc: Large band-winged grasshopper with bright red hindwings visible in crepitating flight. Rocky mountain slopes. Spring flier.
hp: Open grassland. Display flights in courtship. Preys on grasshopper. Acrididae.

desc: Riley's short-winged grasshopper — medium grasshopper with abbreviated wings (cannot fly). On grasses and herbs. Named for pioneering entomologist C.V. Riley.
hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. On Poaceae (grasses). Acrididae.

desc: Large dark cricket with loud continuous chirping song. Males sing from under rocks and debris. Omnivorous. Common on warm nights.
hp: Omnivore. Song important in acoustic ecology. Prey for many vertebrates. Preys on cricket. Gryllidae.

desc: Large wingless katydid of coastal sage scrub. Nocturnal predator/scavenger. Endemic to coastal California.
hp: Predatory; sage. Associated with Black Sage.

desc: Spectacular desert grasshopper with extremely long thread-like antennae (longer than body). California endemic. Nocturnal. Sandy desert.

fm.Color: Pale tan; antennae longer than body

hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. Preys on grasshopper. Desert Long-horned.

desc: Green katydid of coastal sage scrub. Nocturnal. Excellent leaf mimic — broad green wings with subtle vein pattern. Males produce soft chirping song. On native shrubs including buckwheat and sagebrush. California near-endemic.
hp: Mimicry — Batesian or aggressive. Associated with California Sagebrush.

desc: Tiny ground-dwelling mantis. Wingless. Cryptic on soil. Extremely well camouflaged. Related to L. pacifica.
hp: Cryptically camouflaged against habitat substrate. Soil-dwelling.

desc: Pale band-winged grasshopper of sandy desert habitats. Nearly white — excellent camouflage on pale sand. Blue HW.

fm.Color: Pale white — sand-matching; blue HW in flight

hp: Open ground — crackling display flights. Preys on grasshopper.

desc: Medium band-winged grasshopper. Crepitating flight display on rocky slopes. Blue-tinted hindwings.
hp: Open grassland. Display flights in courtship. Preys on grasshopper. Family Acrididae.

desc: Medium katydid of alkali desert habitats. On saltbush and desert scrub. Desert specialist.
hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. Preys on grasshopper. Desert specialist.

desc: Medium green grasshopper on saltbush (Atriplex) in coastal and desert scrub. Host-specific.
hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. Preys on grasshopper. Desert specialist.

desc: Large wingless katydid with distinctive golden-yellow coloring unusual among typically green or brown katydids. Chaparral habitats. Nocturnal. Omnivorous. Limited range — California. One of the more colorful and distinctive local katydids.
hp: Katydid — nocturnal foliage feeder. Song important in acoustic community. Prey for bats. In chaparral habitat.

desc: Dark cricket preferring rocky habitats. Loud chirping song. Under rocks and in crevices.
hp: Omnivore. Song important in acoustic ecology. Prey for many vertebrates. Preys on cricket. Family Gryllidae.

desc: Medium green katydid living exclusively on creosote bush. Leaf-mimic. Desert specialist. Host-specific.
hp: Specialist on creosote bush (larrea tridentata)

desc: California slantface — medium mottled grasshopper of chaparral and coastal sage scrub. Cryptic brown-gray coloring matches soil and rock. Slanted face (oblique profile) diagnostic of subfamily Gomphocerinae. Short-winged, weak flier. On native shrubs.
hp: Grass; sage. Chaparral. Associated with Black Sage. Preys on grasshopper.

desc: Medium humpbacked wingless cricket of moist dark habitats. Under rocks and logs. Very long antennae. Nocturnal scavenger.
hp: Predator of crickets and related Orthoptera. Preys on cricket. Scavenger.

desc: Vermilion camel cricket — tiny reddish-brown camel cricket of chaparral. Nocturnal. Found in leaf litter under shrubs. The reddish coloring is distinctive — most camel crickets are drab brown. Wingless. Long antennae. California near-endemic.
hp: Nocturnal predator. Chaparral habitat.

desc: Tiny pale cricket with scale-like wings. In leaf litter and under debris. Secretive.
hp: Predator of crickets and related Orthoptera. Preys on cricket. In leaf litter.

desc: Large dark field cricket with distinctive long chirp pattern. Important acoustic insect. At lights.
hp: Predator of crickets and related Orthoptera. Preys on cricket. Attracted to lights at night.

desc: Western chaparral katydid — large katydid of dense chaparral. Brown and green. Males produce buzzy nocturnal song. Omnivorous. Strong legs for moving through dense brush. Long ovipositor in females. Native to California chaparral — not found in other habitats.
hp: Acoustic signaler — song/stridulation important in mate location. Prey for bats and birds. In chaparral habitat.

desc: Large dark katydid of foothill and mountain chaparral. Nocturnal. Long narrow wings. Can bite.

fm.Color: Dark sooty brown; long narrow wings

hp: Katydid — nocturnal foliage feeder. Song important in acoustic community. Prey for bats. In chaparral habitat.

desc: Large wingless katydid ENDEMIC to Santa Catalina Island. Nocturnal. Critical conservation concern — extremely restricted range.
hp: Conservation concern — declining habitat or population. See CONSERVATION status. Nocturnal.

desc: Large wingless katydid restricted to coastal bluff habitats. Nocturnal. Heavy-bodied with reduced wings. Extremely limited range along SoCal coastline. Conservation concern due to habitat loss from coastal development. Feeds on native coastal plants.
hp: Conservation concern — declining habitat or population. See CONSERVATION status. Coastal habitat.

desc: Tiny mantis living on ground in chaparral. Flightless. Brown cryptic coloring matches leaf litter. One of smallest mantises.
hp: In leaf litter. Chaparral habitat. Cryptic camouflage.

desc: Tiny earwig. One of the smallest earwigs. Can fly. On compost heaps and in gardens. Predatory. Mostly nocturnal.

fm.Color: Brown; tiny; can fly — unusual for earwigs

hp: Predator of earwigs and soil-dwelling insects. Preys on earwig. In compost.
